## Why would any answer engine rely on a page it could have produced itself?

Google has a name for what survives.NON-COMMODITY CONTENT.

 Experience: first-hand, lived detail
 Expertise: your knowledge, cited
 Authoritativeness: sourced, fact-checked
 Trust: transparent + reviewed

Built around Google's E-E-A-T by an ex-Google search engineer.

A hand-lettered cardboard sign reading 'no AI slop', with a crossed-out symbol

HOW IT WORKS

## ACME.BOT interviews you before it writes.

It does the work. You just weigh in with the take only you have.

FAQ

## Before you ask.

Will it actually sound like me?

That's the whole point. Before it writes, the agent interviews you in chat and builds the piece around your answers: your takes, your stories, your voice. It's not a generic draft with your name on it.

How is this different from ChatGPT or other AI writers?

Those write an average of what already ranks: generic pages an answer engine could produce itself. ACME.BOT does the research, keywords, and SEO, then interviews you for the opinions, stories, and experience only you have. Google calls the result non-commodity content.

How much of my time does it need?

results.title: ACME.BOT Review 2026: Pricing, Features & Alternatives – Slicey Tools
results.content:
Slicey Tools

Submit

Favicon of ACME.BOT

# ACME.BOTReview

## ACME.BOT is an AI agent that interviews you for expertise, then researches, drafts, illustrates, and auto-publishes SEO blog posts to WordPress or Shopify.

Visit ACME.BOT

ACME.BOT is an AI blogging agent that tries to solve the generic-AI-content problem a different way: instead of just prompting a model to write, it interviews the user first to pull out real opinions and experience, then runs research, drafting, illustration, and publishing around that interview. This review covers what's verified from the vendor's own site and what isn't, since no independent review data exists yet.

## Verdict at a glance [...] ## What kind of tool is ACME.BOT?

ACME.BOT bundles four jobs that content teams usually split across tools or freelancers: keyword and topic research, competitor gap analysis, article drafting, and publishing. Its differentiator, per the vendor, is the interview: before writing, the agent asks the user questions to capture opinions and firsthand knowledge, then folds those answers into the draft rather than generating from a bare prompt. A persistent knowledge base lets users upload documents that the agent can cite verbatim in later posts, and a "humanizer" pass is applied to keep the output from reading like typical AI copy.

## How it works [...] ## Pros and cons

Pros

 Interview step is a genuinely different approach to reducing generic AI-content output
 Bundles research, writing, illustration, and publishing instead of requiring separate tools
 Direct WordPress and Shopify publishing with auto-generated SEO metadata
 Free, no-card trial lets you test real output before paying
 Configurable human checkpoints instead of an all-or-nothing autopilot

Cons

 No independent reviews (G2, Trustpilot, Reddit) exist yet to check the vendor's quality claims against
 Introductory pricing is explicitly temporary, with no committed future rate
 Overage credit pricing isn't published
 No annual billing option currently available

## ACME.BOT alternatives

results.title: AI SEO Agent - My experience of running our blog using an SEO agent | ACME.BOT
results.content:
# AI SEO AgentMy experience of running our blog using an SEO agent

Abhishek Iyer also runs the channel on Telegram. He shares his process of planning ahead, having a weekly cadence, and reading through each blog post before he publishes it.

Telegram thread: reviewing a draft — shorter paragraphs, humanize, titles as questions

ACME.BOT's founder and former Google search engineer documents the workflow he uses to run the company blog with ACME.BOT.

Abhishek Iyer

Abhishek Iyer

Founder, ACME.BOT · former Google search engineer

I wrote search systems before ACME.BOT. When I thought of starting up a blog, I could just hired a content mill to write things. Instead, I chose to run it with an agent of our own -- from my phone. [...] Here's what's been up to this point. We're certainly still in the early days (and so far haven't seen any traffic data to showcase), and I just have a process I follow. At a minimum, every post has to clear this bar to get published: would I have enjoyed reading this?
